slides.title: Variables: Named Boxes for Data
slides.body: A **variable** lets you give a value a name so your program can remember it. Instead of repeating `25` everywhere, just write `age` — and Python knows what you mean.
slides.tip: Variables are how programs remember things while they run.

slides.title: Creating a Variable Is Simple
slides.body: Use `=` to assign a value to a name. The name goes on the left, the value on the right — that's it.

slides.title: Variables Can Change Anytime
slides.body: You can reassign a variable whenever you like. Python just remembers the **latest** value — the old one is gone.
slides.tip: score = score + 10 reads the current value, adds 10, then saves it back.

slides.title: Name Them Well
slides.body: Names must start with a **letter or underscore** and can't be a Python keyword. Use `snake_case` for multi-word names — it's the Python way.
slides.tip: snake_case means words joined by underscores: first_name, total_items.

slides.title: You've Got Variables!
slides.body: You can now store, name, and update values in Python. Every program you'll ever write uses this — you just learned one of the most fundamental ideas in coding.
slides.tip: Remember: = assigns a value. == checks if two values are equal.
